questions.q: Which Hodge-number identities are predicted by mirror symmetry between Calabi–Yau 3-folds $X$ and $Y$?
questions.explain: Mirror symmetry swaps the two non-trivial Hodge numbers of a CY 3-fold: Kähler moduli on one side become complex-structure moduli on the other, so $h^{1,1}$ and $h^{2,1}$ trade places.

questions.q: Which physical pairing does the mirror correspondence implement?
questions.explain: Mirror symmetry exchanges symplectic (A-model, type-IIA) data on $X$ with complex (B-model, type-IIB) data on $Y$.

questions.q: What is exchanged at the level of moduli spaces?
choices: Kähler moduli of $X$ ↔ complex-structure moduli of $Y$.
Picard group of $X$ ↔ Brauer group of $Y$.
Fundamental group of $X$ ↔ étale cohomology of $Y$.
questions.explain: The complexified Kähler cone of $X$ (deformations of $\omega$) is identified with the complex-structure moduli of $Y$ (deformations of $J$).

questions.q: What did Candelas–de la Ossa–Green–Parkes compute on the mirror of the quintic?
choices: Periods $\int_\gamma \Omega$ of the holomorphic 3-form, then read off Gromov–Witten invariants of the quintic.
Galois representations attached to the Hasse–Weil zeta function.
Hecke eigenvalues of a weight-2 modular form.
questions.explain: They solved a Picard–Fuchs ODE for the periods on the mirror, applied the mirror map, and matched the resulting series to a genus-zero GW generating function — predicting curve counts on the quintic for every degree.

questions.q: How many lines are there on a generic quintic 3-fold in $\mathbb{P}^4$?
questions.explain: The classical count is $2{,}875$ lines (degree 1 rational curves). $609{,}250$ is the number of conics, $317{,}206{,}375$ the twisted cubics.

questions.q: Who later gave rigorous mathematical proofs (to all genus-zero degrees) of the CdGP predictions?
questions.explain: Givental's mirror theorem (mid-1990s) and the independent proof by Lian, Liu, and Yau put the genus-zero predictions on firm mathematical footing.

questions.q: Genus-zero Gromov–Witten invariants $N_\beta$ of a Calabi–Yau 3-fold $X$ are integrals over which moduli space?
choices: $\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{0,0}(X, \beta)$ of stable maps from genus-zero curves to $X$ in class $\beta$.
questions.explain: Gromov–Witten invariants are virtual integrals over the moduli of stable maps; for a CY 3-fold the virtual dimension at $g=0$ is zero, so the integral of $1$ is a number.

questions.q: What kind of geometric data does the A-model depend on?
questions.explain: Gromov–Witten counts depend on the symplectic class $[\omega]$; varying $J$ doesn't change the invariants. That is the A vs. B asymmetry mirror symmetry exchanges.

questions.q: What does "pseudo-holomorphic" mean in the definition of a stable map $f\colon \Sigma \to X$?
choices: $f$ satisfies $\overline{\partial}_J f = 0$ for a chosen almost-complex structure $J$ tame against $\omega$.
questions.explain: Pseudo-holomorphic means the Cauchy–Riemann equations $\overline{\partial}_J f = 0$ hold for a tame almost-complex structure — Gromov's notion that founded symplectic curve counting.

questions.q: What is a period of a Calabi–Yau 3-fold $Y$?
choices: An eigenvalue of the Laplacian on $Y$.
An integral $\int_\gamma \Omega$ where $\Omega$ is the holomorphic 3-form and $\gamma$ is a 3-cycle.
A trace of Frobenius on étale cohomology.
A character of the fundamental group.
questions.explain: Periods pair the holomorphic top form against integral 3-cycles. As complex structure varies, the periods are holomorphic in $t$ and satisfy the Picard–Fuchs ODE.

choices: A differential equation satisfied by the periods $\Pi_\gamma(t)$ as the complex structure varies.
A Hamiltonian equation for the symplectic form $\omega$.
The heat equation on the moduli space.
questions.explain: Periods of a smooth family of CYs satisfy a linear ODE in the complex-structure parameter — the Picard–Fuchs equation — because $H^3$ has finite rank and Gauss–Manin connects the fibres.

questions.q: What does the mirror map do?
choices: It is a real diffeomorphism between $X$ and $Y$.
It is a change of coordinates from B-model parameters $t$ to A-model parameters $q$ identifying period integrals on $Y$ with GW generating functions on $X$.
It takes Hodge classes to algebraic classes.
It sends prime ideals of $\mathbb{Z}$ to maximal ideals of $\mathbb{C}[t]$.
questions.explain: The mirror map is the canonical change of variables on the moduli side that turns hypergeometric period series into Gromov–Witten potentials, making the prediction explicit.

questions.q: Kontsevich's homological mirror symmetry conjectures an equivalence of which two categories?
questions.explain: HMS pairs the bounded derived category of coherent sheaves on the B-side with the (split-closed) Fukaya category on the A-side, both viewed as triangulated (or $A_\infty$) categories.

questions.q: Objects of the Fukaya category of $(Y, \omega)$ are (roughly):
choices: Lagrangian submanifolds of $Y$ equipped with brane data, with hom-spaces given by Lagrangian intersection Floer cohomology.
questions.explain: The Fukaya category is built from Lagrangians (with extra data — orientation, spin, local system, grading), and morphisms are computed by Floer chain complexes counting pseudo-holomorphic strips.

questions.q: Which case of HMS is among the earliest to be proven?
choices: The case of arbitrary smooth projective 5-folds.
The elliptic curve $E$ vs. its mirror dual elliptic curve (Polishchuk–Zaslow).
Singular projective varieties of arbitrary dimension.
questions.explain: Polishchuk and Zaslow proved HMS for elliptic curves in 1998; the quartic K3 (Seidel) and the quintic 3-fold (Sheridan, after Nadler–Zaslow's work on cotangent bundles) followed.

questions.q: What geometric structure on a CY 3-fold $X$ does the SYZ conjecture posit?
choices: A holomorphic foliation of $X$ by Riemann surfaces.
A special-Lagrangian $T^3$-fibration $X \to B$ over a real 3-dimensional base.
A finite Galois cover of $X$ by an abelian variety.
questions.explain: The conjectured fibration has generic fibres that are 3-tori, special-Lagrangian (calibrated by $\mathrm{Re}\,\Omega$) — the geometric core of the SYZ picture.

questions.q: How is the mirror $Y$ constructed in the SYZ picture?
choices: Replace each fibre $T^3_b$ by its dual torus $\check T^3_b = \mathrm{Hom}(T^3_b, U(1))$ and glue.
questions.explain: $Y \to B$ is the same base equipped with the dual torus fibration; on each fibre this is T-duality, exchanging position and momentum on the torus and trading symplectic for complex structure.

questions.q: Which programme builds CY mirror pairs from tropical / integral-affine bases via SYZ-style fibrations?
questions.explain: Gross and Siebert reconstruct CY mirror pairs from integral-affine bases with singularities, building toric degenerations and using log/tropical methods — a programme rooted in the SYZ conjecture.
